question: Explain the difference between Entity Type and Entity Set with a suitable example.
expectedPoints: Entity Type is the abstract definition or blueprint defining common attributes (e.g., STUDENT with roll_no, name, age)
Entity Set is the actual collection of entities at a given point in time (e.g., all students currently enrolled in a college)
Entity type defines structure; entity set contains actual instances

question: Define the following attributes with examples: (i) Simple (ii) Composite (iii) Multi-valued (iv) Derived
expectedPoints: Simple: atomic indivisible attribute (e.g., age, salary)
Composite: can be divided (e.g., address = street, city, zip)
Multi-valued: multiple values (e.g., phone_numbers)
Derived: computed from others (e.g., age from DOB)

question: What is a Weak Entity Type? How is it different from a Strong Entity Type?
expectedPoints: Weak entity depends on strong entity for identification, has no key of its own
Strong entity has its own primary key and exists independently
Weak entity identified by partial key + owner's primary key
Examples: DEPENDENT (weak) vs EMPLOYEE (strong)

question: Explain Cardinality and Participation constraints in ER model.
expectedPoints: Cardinality: maximum number of associations (1:1, 1:N, M:N)
Participation: whether entity must/can participate (total/mandatory or partial/optional)
Cardinality shown by lines (1 or N)
Participation shown by single/double line connecting entity to relationship

question: Draw and explain the ER diagram notation for: (i) Simple attribute (ii) Composite attribute (iii) Multi-valued attribute (iv) Derived attribute (v) Key attribute
expectedPoints: Simple: single ellipse
Composite: ellipse with connected sub-ellipses
Multi-valued: double ellipse
Derived: dashed ellipse
Key: underline the attribute name

question: What is a key attribute in ER modeling? Explain its importance with an example.
expectedPoints: Key attribute uniquely identifies each entity
Must have unique, non-null values
Primary key constraint ensures no duplicates
Example: roll_no in STUDENT, account_no in BANK

question: Consider a BANK database with entities CUSTOMER and ACCOUNT. What structural constraints would you define for the relationship 'has' between them?
expectedPoints: Cardinality: One CUSTOMER can have multiple ACCOUNTs (1:N)
Participation: CUSTOMER participation may be partial (can exist without account)
ACCOUNT participation is total (must belong to a customer)
Each ACCOUNT must have a CUSTOMER (mandatory)

question: Explain the Entity-Relationship model in detail. Discuss the concepts of Entity Types, Entity Sets, and various types of attributes with suitable examples and ER diagram notations.
expectedPoints: ER model proposed by Peter Chen in 1976
Entity: real-world object that can be distinctly identified
Entity Type: collection of entities sharing common attributes (blueprint)
Entity Set: actual collection of entities at a point in time
Attributes types: Simple (atomic), Composite (divisible), Single-valued, Multi-valued, Derived (computed)
Key attribute for unique identification
ER diagram notations: rectangles for entities, ellipses for attributes, diamonds for relationships

question: What are Structural Constraints in ER model? Explain Cardinality and Participation constraints in detail with examples and diagram representations.
expectedPoints: Structural constraints define rules for relationships
Cardinality: maximum associations - 1:1, 1:N, M:N
Participation: minimum participation - total (mandatory) or partial (optional)
1:1 example: one student has one library card
1:N example: one department has many employees
M:N example: students enroll in many courses
Total participation shown by double line
Partial participation shown by single line

question: Differentiate between Strong and Weak Entity Types. With examples, explain how weak entities are identified and represented in ER diagrams.
expectedPoints: Strong entity: has primary key, exists independently
Weak entity: no key of own, depends on owner entity
Weak entity identified by: partial key + owner's primary key
Weak entity shown by double rectangle
Partial key (discriminator) shown by dashed underline
Example: ORDER (strong) has LINE_ITEM (weak)
Relationship between owner and weak entity is always identifying relationship (double diamond)

question: Design an ER diagram for a UNIVERSITY database system with the following requirements: Students, Professors, Departments, and Courses. Include appropriate attributes, identify key attributes, define relationships with cardinality and participation constraints.
expectedPoints: Entities: STUDENT(roll_no, name, dob), PROFESSOR(emp_id, name, specialization)
DEPARTMENT(dept_id, name, budget), COURSE(course_code, title, credits)
Relationships: PROFESSOR teaches COURSE (1:N), STUDENT enrolls COURSE (M:N)
DEPARTMENT has PROFESSOR (1:N), DEPARTMENT offers COURSE (1:N)
Key attributes: roll_no, emp_id, dept_id, course_code
Cardinality and participation constraints appropriately defined
